docs.title: Wong Kin Chong O/A level Notes
docs.text: Welcome to my notes website. I am Kin Chong, currently studying Computer Science in NUS. On the side, I give tuition for O level and A level students on math and sciences.

docs.title: What's this website for
docs.text:
This website is created to support my students in their revision and independent learning. It provides an alternative set of notes for those who may find school materials insufficient or difficult to follow.

At the same time, I believe that education should be accessible to everyone. Not all students can afford tuition, so this platform is open to anyone who wishes to learn, revise, and improve at their own pace.

In the future, I hope to expand this website to include more features, such as a discussion forum, progress tracking, quizzes, and interactive visualisations. However, with current academic commitments, development may take some time. Thank you for your patience and support.

docs.title: How to Use This Site
docs.text:
1. Open the sidebar
  2. Choose a subject
  3. Choose a chapter and read
  4. share with friends if you find this helpful :)

docs.title: Atoms and Subatomic Particles
docs.text:
Learning Outcomes 1(a) identify and describe protons, neutrons and electrons in terms of their relative charges and relative masses 1(c) describe the distribution of mass and charges within an atom 1(d) deduce the numbers of protons, neutrons and electrons present in both atoms and ions given proton and nucleon numbers (and charge)

An atom is the smallest component of an element having the chemical properties of the element.

All atoms are made of up the same three subatomic particles

  • eletrons
  • protons
  • neutrons

The protons and neutrons make up the nucleus of the atom. Thus they are also referred to as nucleons. The electrons are surrounding the nucleus.

docs.title: Relative Mass and Charge of Subatomic Particles
docs.text: property proton electron neutron relative charge +1 -1 0 relative mass 1 \(\frac{1}{1836}\) 1

docs.title: Distribution of Mass and Charge in an Atom
docs.text:
So far, we know that

  • The protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus
  • The electrons have negligible mass compared to the protons and neutrons
  • The protons are positively charged
  • The electrons are negatively charged
  • The neutrons are neutral

Therefore, we can say that 99% of the mass is concentrated in the nucleus. All the positive charges are in the nucleus and the negative charges are on the electrons.

docs.title: Nuclide Notation
docs.text:
\[^{A}_{Z}\mathrm{X}\]

The proton number (Z) is the number of protons in the nucleus of each atom of an element.

In the atom, the number of electrons is also equal to the number of protons since atoms are neutral

The nucleon number (A), also called the mass number, is the total number of neutrons and protons in the nucleus of an atom of an element.

docs.title: Deflection of ions and charged particles in Electric Field
docs.text: Learning Outcome 1(b) deduce the behaviour of beams of protons, neutrons and electrons in an electric field

docs.title: Ions
docs.text:
A negative ion (anion) has more electrons than protons.

A positive ion (cation) has more protons than electrons.

docs.title: What happens when particles pass through electric field
docs.text:
Since like charges repel and unlike charges attract, negatively charged particles such as electrons will be attracted to the positive plate while positively charged particles such as protons will be attracted to the negative plate.

Neutron particles such as neutrons experience no attraction/repulsion and thus are not deflected.

However, if you have not realised, the proton and electron are deflected by different angles! More precise, angle 1 (electron) is greater than angle 2 (proton)

The reason is simple and requires a tiny bit of Physics.

Both the electron and proton carry charge of the same magnitude of 1. Thus they experience forces of the same magnitude!

Therefore, the one with a smaller mass will deflect by a larger angle (imagine if you push an elephant and a ball with the same force, the elephant won't move a inch and the ball will fly!).

Since we know that electrons have a relative mass is \(\frac{1}{1836}\) while that of protons is 1, the electrons will deflect by a much greater angle.

To summaries all this

\[ angle \propto \frac{charge}{mass} \]

docs.title: Isotopes
docs.text:
Learning Outcome 1(e) (ii)distinguish between isotopes on the basis of different numbers of neutrons present

Isotopes are atoms of an element that have the same proton number but different nucleon numbers (or same number of protons and different number of neutrons).

Isotopes of an element have the same electronic configuration and thus have similar chemical properties (undergo similar reactions).

They have different relative isotopic masses and thus different physical properties (density etc).

docs.title: Learning Outcome
docs.text: Learning Outcome 1(a) identify and describe protons, neutrons and electrons in terms of their relative charges and relative masses 1(b) deduce the behaviour of beams of protons, neutrons and electrons in an electric field 1(c) describe the distribution of mass and charges within an atom 1(d) deduce the numbers of protons, neutrons and electrons present in both atoms and ions given proton and nucleon numbers (and charge) 1(e) (i) describe the contribution of protons and neutrons to atomic nuclei in terms of proton number and nucleon number  (ii)distinguish between isotopes on the basis of different numbers of neutrons present 1(f) describe the number and relative energies of the s, p and d orbitals for the principal quantum numbers 1, 2 and 3 and also the 4s and 4p orbitals 1(g) describe the shapes of s, p and d orbitals (see also Section 13) [knowledge of wave functions is not required] 1(h) state the electronic configuration of atoms and ions given the proton number (and charge) 1(i) explain the factors influencing the ionisation energies of elements (see the Data Booklet) (see also Section 5) 1(j) deduce the electronic configurations of elements from successive ionisation energy data 1(k) interpret successive ionisation energy data of an element in terms of the position of that element within the Periodic Table 5(a) recognise variation in the electronic configurations across a Period and down a Group 5(b) describe and explain qualitatively the general trends and variations in atomic radius, ionic radius, first ionisation energy and electronegativity:  (i) across a Period in terms of shielding and nuclear charge  (ii) down a Group in terms of increasing number of electron shells, shielding and nuclear charge 6(a) define the terms relative atomic, isotopic, molecular and formula mass 6(c) calculate the relative atomic mass of an element given the relative abundances of its isotopes 13(a) explain that a transition element is a d block element whose atom has an incomplete d subshell, or which can give rise to cations with an incomplete d subshell 13(b) state the electronic configuration of a first row transition element and its ions (see also 1(h)) 13(c) explain why atomic radii and first ionisation energies of the transition elements are relatively invariant

docs.title: Relative Masses
docs.text: Learning Outcomes 6(a) define the terms relative atomic, isotopic, molecular and formula mass 6(c) calculate the relative atomic mass of an element given the relative abundances of its isotopes

docs.title: Relative Atomic Mass
docs.text:
\[ Relative \; atomic \; mass, A_{r} = \; \frac{average \; mass \; of \; one \; atom \; of \; an \; element}{\frac{1}{12} \; \times \; the \; mass \; of \; one \; atom \; of \; ^{12}C} \]

The relative mass of atom of an element is the ratio of the average mass of one atom of the element to \(\frac{1}{12}\) times the mass of one atom of \(^{12}C\)

docs.title: Relative Isotopic Mass
docs.text:
\[ Relative \; isotopic \; mass, \; A_{r} \; = \; \frac{average \; mass \; of \; an \; isotope \; of \; an \; element}{\frac{1}{12} \; \times \; the \; mass \; of \; one \; atom \; of \; ^{12}C} \]

The relative mass of an isotope of an element is the ratio of the average mass of an isotope of the element to \(\frac{1}{12}\) times the mass of one atom of \(^{12}C\)

docs.title: Relative Abundance of Isotopes
docs.text:
The percentage of a particular isotope compared to the total number of atoms of that element in a mixture of isotopes is called the isotopic abundance or the relative abundance.

\[ Relative \, Atomic \, Mass, \, A_{r} \; = \; \sum (relative \; isotopic \; mass \; \times \; isotopic \; abundance) \]
